Key Idea: Meaning is organized in frames; words evoke frames
Challenges SRE: Partial
Citation: Fillmore, C. J. (1982). Frame Semantics. In Linguistics in the Morning Calm.

Paper: Lakoff (1987) Women Fire and Dangerous Things
Key Idea: Categories are prototype-based not definitional; cognitive models structure meaning
Challenges SRE: Partial
Citation: Lakoff, G. (1987). Women, Fire, and Dangerous Things.

Key Idea: Grammar is symbolic; form-meaning pairings at multiple levels of schematicity
Challenges SRE: Partial
Citation: Langacker, R. W. (1987). Foundations of Cognitive Grammar.

Key Idea: Force dynamics underlie causal language; semantic structure is topological
Challenges SRE: No - SRE affordances are implicitly force-dynamic but not formalized as such
Citation: Talmy, L. (2000). Toward a Cognitive Semantics.

Key Idea: Language acquired through intention-reading and pattern-finding in usage events
Challenges SRE: No - SRE assumes authored experiences not naturalistic input
Citation: Tomasello, M. (2003). Constructing a Language.

Key Idea: English nouns/verbs organized into synsets with hypernym/hyponym relations
Challenges SRE: Yes - WordNet is lexical not semantic; no affordances or cross-lingual projection
Citation: Miller, G. A. (1995). WordNet. Communications of the ACM.

Key Idea: Corpus-based resource documenting semantic frames and their lexical units
Challenges SRE: Partial - FrameNet frames are richer than SRE affordances but not deployable as learning apps
Citation: Baker et al (1998). The Berkeley FrameNet Project.

Key Idea: Open multilingual semantic graph from crowd-sourced and expert knowledge
Challenges SRE: Yes - ConceptNet trades provenance for scale; SRE makes opposite choice
Citation: Speer et al (2017). ConceptNet 5.5. AAAI 2017.

Key Idea: Automatic multilingual semantic network from WordNet + Wikipedia
Challenges SRE: Yes - BabelNet automates cross-lingual mapping; SRE requires human-authored mappings
Citation: Navigli & Ponzetto (2012). BabelNet. AI Journal.

Key Idea: Million-assertion formal ontology of common sense
Challenges SRE: SRE is not designed for general common-sense reasoning; Cyc's formalism is too heavy for language documentation
Citation: Lenat, D. B. (1995). Cyc. Communications of the ACM.

Key Idea: Upper ontology providing formal foundation for WordNet
Challenges SRE: SRE uses minimal flat categories; SUMO is philosophically rigorous but impractical for non-specialist contributors
Citation: Niles & Pease (2001). Towards a Standard Upper Ontology. FOIS.

Key Idea: Descriptive ontology distinguishing enduring/perduring
Challenges SRE: No
Citation: SRE categories are pragmatic not philosophical; DOLCE distinctions not needed for language learning
Source: Gangemi et al (2002). Sweetening Ontologies with DOLCE. EKAW.

Key Idea: ~3000 of ~7000 languages are endangered; documentation urgency
Challenges SRE: No limitations identified
Citation: Moseley, C. (ed.) (2010). Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger. UNESCO.

Key Idea: A language disappears every few weeks; linguists must act urgently
Challenges SRE: No limitations identified
Citation: Krauss, M. (1992). Language. 68(1).

Paper: Hinton (2001) Green Book of Language Revitalisation
Key Idea: Revitalization must create speakers not just archives; community-driven approaches
Challenges SRE: No - SRE's experience model aligns with creating speakers
Citation: Hinton, L. (2001). In Hinton & Hale (eds.), The Green Book.

Key Idea: Linguistic diversity is a human value; endangered languages lose irreplaceable knowledge
Challenges SRE: No limitations identified
Citation: Hale, K. (1992). Language. 68(1).

Key Idea: GIDS scale classifies endangerment stages; reversing shift requires intergenerational transmission
Challenges SRE: Partial - SRE targets Stage 7-8 but cannot replace intergenerational transmission
Citation: Fishman, J. A. (1991). Reversing Language Shift.

Key Idea: Technology must respect community data sovereignty; one-size-fits-all platforms fail
Challenges SRE: Partial - SRE addresses sovereignty via layered licensing but requires technical literacy
Citation: Holton, G. (2011). In Austin & Sallabank (eds.), Cambridge Handbook.

Key Idea: Language acquired through comprehensible input (i+1); not through explicit grammar instruction
Challenges SRE: Partial - SRE semantic density metric approximates comprehensibility but has not been validated empirically
Citation: Krashen, S. D. (1985). The Input Hypothesis.

Key Idea: Comprehensible output is also necessary; learners must produce language not just understand it
Challenges SRE: Partial - Entity reuse ratio encourages repeated production but SRE does not enforce output
Citation: Swain, M. (2005). In Hinkel (ed.), Handbook of Research in SLA.

Key Idea: Balanced course needs meaning input + output + language-focused learning + fluency
Challenges SRE: No - SRE experience types map to strands but mapping is advisory not compiler-enforced
Citation: Nation, I. S. P. (2007). Innovation in Language Learning and Teaching.

Key Idea: Survey of documentation practices; gap between documentation and usable resources
Challenges SRE: Partial - Paper identifies the gap SRE addresses but does not endorse compiler-based approaches
Citation: Austin & Sallabank (eds.) (2011). Cambridge Handbook of Endangered Languages.

Key Idea: Seven dimensions of portability; tool-specific formats impede data reuse
Challenges SRE: Partial - SRE uses simple YAML/JSON but has not demonstrated import from existing tools
Citation: Bird & Simons (2003). Language. 79(3).

Key Idea: Perception is direct; organisms perceive affordances (possibilities for action)
Challenges SRE: No - Affordance concept is foundational but Gibson's theory is about visual perception not language
Citation: Gibson, J. J. (1979). The Ecological Approach to Visual Perception.
